retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Human facial cosmetic evaluation advances as 3D technology improves. Currently, providers use 2D photos that complement both 2D and 3D radiographs. Photos are taken with the patient looking directly at the camera, at a 45° angle, and in profile view. Each photo allows facial volume visualization from different angles. The lateral cephalogram should be well studied and analyzed, as it contains critical data used for facial analysis. Well-known facial bone and soft tissue relationships are determined from this radiograph. The chin's position and the lower facial structural relationships may be classified based on numerous measurements. Frequently used parameters include the following: Holdaway Ratio (see Image. Holdaway Ratio) Zero Degree Meridian Line (see Image. Zero Degree Meridian) True Vertical Line (see Image. True Vertical Line) Labiomental Angle (see Image. Labiomental Angle)

retrieved_docs.title: Transconjunctival Blepharoplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Markings
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be marked sitting up. The outlines of the three fat pads are marked, and a topographical marking is made over the individual pads, together with notes if one pad or side needs more or less attention. Careful attention is paid to the nasojugal groove and the malar groove. In most patients undergoing straight transconjunctival blepharoplasty, these grooves will not be prominent. The presence of prominent nasojugal and malar grooves indicate different surgical procedures. Sometimes, subtle grooves may be present: these should be marked in case they need filling with the patient's own fat or fillers, during or after a transconjunctival lower blepharoplasty.

retrieved_docs.title: Autologous Fat Grafting for Facial Rejuvenation -- Technique or Treatment -- Consideration of Facial Aesthetic Units
retrieved_docs.content: Glabella/nose: The most common features to be addressed in this region include the glabellar vertical rhytids ("frown lines" or "number 11s") or a deep radix in order to camouflage a dorsal hump. However, any contour irregularity of the nose may be addressed. The cannula entry point can be in the forehead, medial eyebrow, cheek, alar base, nasal tip, or upper cutaneous lip. Various cannulas may be employed, although the 19 ga Coleman type 3 cannula is commonly used to layer the fat from the periosteum to the intradermal layers. Fat can be injected posterior to the nasal cartilage for expansion of the internal valve, to fill concavities in the radix or within a saddle deformity, or to smooth contour irregularities after rhinoplasty or trauma. [16] Regarding the glabella, one must be careful to remain superficial (some advocate intradermal injection) and avoid intravascular injection to minimize the risk of blindness and/or stroke.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The MCA is commonly defined as the angle between a line passing through the nasal tip and pogonion and a tangential line through the submental point, with an ideal angle ranging from 110º to 120º. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhombic Flaps -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After elevation, the flap is transposed into the defect. The donor site should be closed first by bringing point F to point C (see Image. Dufourmental Rhombic Flap Modification); this is the point of maximum tension during closure. The remainder of the closure should be completed in 2 layers. On the face, buried, interrupted 5-0 poliglecaprone sutures in the deep dermis and simple, interrupted 6-0 polypropylene sutures on the skin surface are typically used. Suppose a standing cutaneous cone deformity arises at the pivot point near the base of the flap, point D (see Image. Dufourmental Rhombic Flap Modification). In that case, additional skin may be excised to correct the contour, ensuring the flap's base is not narrowed excessively to compromise circulation. A rule of thumb for a random pattern local flap on the face is that the width of the base should be at least one-third of the flap's length. On the trunk or extremities, the width-length ratio for a local flap is greater, at 1:2, due to relatively poorer cutaneous perfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Frontal Cranioplasty with Brow Lift and Hairline Adjustment
retrieved_docs.content: In the rare cases that the frontal sinus is absent or the bone of the anterior table is exceptionally thick, simple burring of the bone may produce the desired contour (type 1 frontal cranioplasty) and obviate the need for removal and recession of the anterior table (type 3 frontal cranioplasty). Instead of a formal type 3 cranioplasty, some surgeons prefer to bur the bone of the anterior table down until it is extremely thin and then fracture it gently with a mallet to achieve the desired contour; this technique may be more effective when the intersinus septum is absent or very thin because the septa limit the extent to which the anterior table bone can be recessed.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The Holdaway ratio compares the chin's bony position to the lower incisor tips. This ratio involves 4 points: the pogonion (Po), lower incisor tip (L1), nasion (N), and B point (B). Po is the bony chin's most anterior aspect. B is the anterior mandible's most concave aspect. N denotes the nasofrontal suture area . To get the Holdaway ratio, the vertical line connecting N to B is measured. The distance in millimeters from Po to N-B and L1 to N-B should be equal, ie, must have a 1:1 ratio. A deviation of more than 4 mm warrants correction. Excessive lower incisor proclination increases this ratio and distorts accurate analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Facial implants may also augment the posterior mandible in the transverse dimension. The bigonial width is the distance between the right and left mandibles' most inferior, lateral, and posterior edges. Farkas described the average bigonial widths of female and male individuals of Caucasian descent in relation to their bizygomatic width. The male bigonial width was found to be 34 mm shorter than their bizygomatic width. In contrast, the female bigonial width was found to be 36 mm shorter than their bizygomatic width.

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Facial fat pads (males commonly have fuller buccal fat pads and females have more malar fat volume)

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Merrifield adopted a similar yet slightly different method for chin analysis by introducing the Z-angle. This angle is formed by the Frankfort horizontal line and a line extending from the pogonion to the most anterior projection point of the lips. Ideally, the Z-angle should fall within the range of 75° to 85°. Legan introduced an angle-based approach for chin analysis, consisting of a line from the glabella to the subnasal point and another from the subnasal point to the pogonion. The ideal value for this angle is 12°, with an acceptable range between 8° and 16. [4] [5] [6] [7] [8] Finally, for comprehensive chin analysis, the cervicomental angle (CMA) and the mentocervical angle (MCA) are crucial facial angles to consider. The CMA is formed by a tangential line from the submental point and another tangential line to the neck at the subcervical intersection or the lowest point between the submental area and the neck. Ideally, the CMA measures 121º for men and 126º for women.

retrieved_docs.title: Lip Implants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lip surface area: the most attractive lip augmentation should create roughly a 50% increase in surface area from baseline. Note: The lip surface area should equal approximately 10% of the height of the lower third of the face. Lip vertical height (assessed on frontal view): the upper to lower lip height ratio should be between 1.6:1 and 2:1 (i.e., the lower lip should be taller than the upper lip). Lip projection (assessed on lateral view): referencing a straight line from the subnasale to the pogonion, the upper lip projection should be 3.5 mm anterior to the line and the lower lip 2.2 mm, roughly equal to a 1.6:1 ratio (i.e., the upper lip projections should be slightly more than the lower lip) Lip width: should be approximately 40% of the width of the lower face

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Genioplasty
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction of chin width and protrusion can significantly improve the femininity of the lower third of the face. [40] As with mandibular angle reduction, genioplasty can be performed via either intraoral or extraoral incisions. The advantage to the intraoral incision is that it avoids an external scar, but it does place the mental nerves at a higher risk than a submental incision would. The mental nerves exit the anterior mandible through foramina inferior to and between the first and second bicuspids, which generally falls on the mid pupillary line, below the supraorbital and infraorbital notches, and foramina.

retrieved_docs.title: Transconjunctival Blepharoplasty -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Transconjunctival blepharoplasty is undertaken to improve the appearance of lower eyelids. Specifically, it aims to reduce or efface the bulges created by the medial, central, and lateral fat pads. In its purest form, the procedure is indicated in the younger patients who have isolated prominence of these fat pads without significant lower eyelid laxity, without skin or orbicularis redundancy, and without festoons. The patient should not have significant nasojugal or malar grooves or cheek ptosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: With respect to gender affirmation procedures for the face, the majority of interventions will occur in patients transitioning from male to female, i.e., transgender women. While there are slightly more transgender women than transgender men in the population (33% transgender women, 29% transgender men, 35% non-binary, 3% crossdressers, according to the USTS), the reason that more females require surgery than males is that testosterone therapy typically produces enough changes in secondary sex characteristics of the face (growth of facial hair, thickening of the skin, increase in frontal bossing, lowering of the voice, etc.) that surgery is not necessary. [2] [3] In some cases, placement of implants or fat transfer can increase volume in the lower third of the face and contribute to masculinization. Still, the primary area of focus for facial feminization is generally the upper third. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Gonzalez-Ulloa proposed a line that runs perpendicular to the Frankfort horizontal line and passes through the nasion (the deepest portion of radix). According to this method, the pogonion should either intersect with this line or be situated immediately posterior to it. Furthermore, he classified the degree of insufficiency into 3 grades—grade I if within 1 cm posterior to this line, grade II between 1 and 2 cm posterior, and grade III if greater than 2 cm posterior. Silver proposed a line perpendicular to the Frankfort horizontal line, crossing through the mucocutaneous junction of the lower lip. According to his suggestion, the pogonion should align with this line or, ideally for women, be within 2 mm posterior to it.

retrieved_docs.title: Extended SMAS Facelift -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After the skin flap is raised, the SMAS is incised and elevated. In the technique described by Quatela and colleagues, this incision is made vertically, approximately 2 cm anterior to the tragus, which allows for the development of a plane deep to the SMAS and inferior to the zygomatic arch, as well as the elevation of a thin, inferiorly based SMAS flap. This finger or tongue of SMAS is subsequently transposed beneath the ear and suspended posteriorly onto the mastoid, applying tension along the vector of the mandibular margin to efface the jowls (see Images. Inferiorly Based SMAS Flap, Extending from Zygomatic Arch to Mandibular Angle and SMAS Flap Transposition to Mastoid, Tightening Mandible Contour and Effacing Jowl). [6] Continuing the sub-SMAS plane of dissection anteriorly elevates the orbicularis oculi muscle and the malar fat pad into the skin flap and exposes the superficial surface of the zygomaticus major muscle. Doing so allows lifting of the midfacial fat, adding a deep-plane component to the extended SMAS facelift without manipulating tissue in the vicinity of the frontal branch of the facial nerve, which passes over the middle third of the zygomatic arch (see Image . Deep Plane Rhytidectomy). [21] This maneuver is considered an integral part of the extended SMAS rhytidectomy technique by some authors. In contrast, others view it as an optional addition that makes the lift a hybrid procedure suitable for certain patients. [7] [6] Most of the sub-SMAS dissection can be carried out with careful spreading of facelift scissors or the Trepsat facial dissectors. Regardless of whether the malar fat pad is addressed during the procedure, dividing the zygomatic and masseteric cutaneous ligaments is crucial for mobilizing the SMAS sufficiently to achieve a cosmetically pleasing and long-lasting lift. Once platysmal muscle fibers are identified at the inferior extent of the incision, blunt dissection may extend in this sub-SMAS or submuscular plane past the body of the mandible and into the neck, exposing the submandibular glands. These glands may require judicious debulking to prevent submandibular fullness after the platysma is suspended. However, care must be taken to avoid injury to the marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve during this maneuver. This subplatysmal elevation places the facial vessels and the lower division of the facial nerve at risk of injury, which is why this dissection is typically performed bluntly. Alternatively, the cervical dissection may be performed in a subcutaneous plane to reduce the risk of nerve injury and facilitate the division of the mandibular cutaneous ligaments that produce marionette lines. If a subcutaneous neck elevation is employed, an additional short submental incision permits access to the anterior digastric muscles and central subplatysmal or submental fat, which can then be reduced as necessary. Over-resection of submental fat should be avoided, as it may cause prominence of the anterior digastric muscles and result in a cobra-neck deformity. Anteriorly, the sub-SMAS dissection ends lateral to the oral commissure, affording access to the buccal fat pad, or boule of Bichat, for debulking if necessary. Care must be taken when addressing the buccal fat pad, as the distal midfacial branches of the facial nerve course superficially over it.

retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that is linked hierarchically to the more general term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the former is a child concept of the latter. This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. Both the prolapsed arm and the transverse/oblique presentations are also

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category of fetal malposition. Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which specific abnormal fetal presentations are organized under generic presentation terms, which in turn are subsumed by the overarching classification of malposition; malpresentation, a key concept for understanding and managing unstable fetal positions during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 963
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is an instance of the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that this fetal presentation can occur regardless of the specific clinical episode. The “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” category is classified as a type of “Malposition; malpresentation,” placing it within the overall group of abnormal fetal positions. Within the same “Malposition; malpresentation” group, other specific abnormalities are also classified, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The specific entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is linked to the Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” confirming that it refers to a fetal arm that has prolapsed. Additionally, “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is an instance of the broader “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” further illustrating the hierarchical organization of these malpresentation categories. Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy in which face or brow presentations, prolapsed fetal arms, and transverse or oblique presentations are all subtypes of malposition or malpresentation, each linked to more general descriptive categories that are independent of specific episodes of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: The fetal presentation entities “Face OR brow presentation of fetus” and “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” are each linked to more generic descriptors—“Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” respectively—indicating that the specific presentations are sub‑categories of broader, episode‑agnostic classifications. Both of these broader presentation categories are classified under the overarching obstetric condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which groups together abnormal fetal positions that deviate from the normal cephalic presentation. “Malposition; malpresentation” itself is identified as an indication for obstetric management within the domain “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” highlighting that any malpresentation warrants specific clinical attention during prenatal care, labor, and delivery planning. Within the “Malposition; malpresentation” group, two additional sub‑types are recognized: “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The prolapsed‑arm condition also has a parent term expressed in Arabic, “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” which serves as the linguistic equivalent of the English descriptor. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that specific fetal malpresentations—face or brow presentation, transverse or oblique presentation, and prolapsed fetal arm—are organized under the general category of malposition/malpresentation, which is a key indication for specialized obstetric care throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This structured classification aids clinicians in recognizing, documenting, and managing abnormal fetal positions to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 614
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is organized under the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” which in turn is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation. This malposition; malpresentation category is a central classification within “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” indicating that fetal malpositions are important considerations in obstetric management. Malposition; malpresentation also classifies other specific fetal position abnormalities, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” has the parent term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” the Arabic expression for a prolapsed fetal arm, while “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is linked to its parent “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Additionally, the relationship “has parent” connects the concept of malposition; malpresentation with the Arabic phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين,” which translates to “instability of fetal position,” reinforcing that these malpresentations represent unstable fetal orientations. Collectively, these entities and their hierarchical relationships delineate how various fetal presentations—face or brow, prolapsed arm, transverse or oblique—are categorized under the overarching malposition; malpresentation framework, highlighting their relevance for clinical decision‑making during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1064
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus, also described as Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, is a specific type of fetal malposition that is classified under the broader category Malposition; malpresentation. This malposition is directly relevant to Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, which lists Malposition; malpresentation as its parent concept. Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable (Arabic: تدلّي ذراع الجنين) and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus, with its corresponding unspecified presentation category (Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable), are likewise subtypes classified as Malposition; malpresentation, each having a parent relationship to their specific presentation descriptors. Unspecified malposition or malpresentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, is linked to the broader relationship of Malposition; malpresentation, reflecting the overall instability of fetal position (Arabic: عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Collectively, Malposition; malpresentation serves as the parent category that classifies these various fetal presentations—including Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, and Transverse or oblique presentation—providing a hierarchical framework that guides clinical assessment and management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: Face or brow presentation of fetus, recorded as “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” is classified under the broader category of malposition; malpresentation. This malposition; malpresentation grouping also encompasses other specific fetal presentation abnormalities such as Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable (Arabic: تدلّي ذراع الجنين) and Transverse or oblique presentation of fetus, which is linked to “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” All of these specific presentations are considered sub‑categories of malposition; malpresentation, as indicated by the “has parent” and “classified_as” relationships. The general concept of fetal presentation finding is related to malposition; malpresentation through a broader relationship, and the clinical context for these findings is provided by Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, which also lists malposition; malpresentation as a parent condition. Collectively, these relationships describe a hierarchical classification system in which face or brow presentation, prolapsed fetal arm, and transverse/oblique presentations are all forms of fetal malposition, reflecting instability of fetal position (Arabic: عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين) and guiding obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2138
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is linked to the parent category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This parent category is classified as Malposition; malpresentation, which in turn is classified under the broader category Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. Likewise, Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus is associated with the parent category Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. These hierarchical relationships illustrate that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are subtypes of malposition/malpresentation and are organized within a broader classification of transverse or oblique presentations, providing a structured framework for understanding fetal malpresentations in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 946
retrieved_docs.content: Face or brow presentation of the fetus is a specific type of fetal malposition or malpresentation. It is classified under the broader category of “Malposition; malpresentation,” which also encompasses other abnormal presentations such as prolapsed arm of the fetus and transverse or oblique presentation of the fetus. The prolapsed arm of the fetus (تدلّي ذراع الجنين) and the transverse or oblique presentation are each distinct sub‑categories of malpresentation, with the Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين” serving as the parent concept for the prolapsed arm and the phrase “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” serving as the parent for the transverse/oblique presentation. In the obstetric care hierarchy, “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” is linked to both a previous cesarean delivery (عملية قيصرية سابقة) and to the general category of malposition/malpresentation, indicating that abnormal fetal presentations are important considerations when planning care for women with a prior cesarean scar. Additionally, maternal care for other types of scar from a previous cesarean delivery is classified as a previous cesarean delivery, reinforcing the connection between scar‑related maternal care and the need to assess fetal presentation. Overall, fetal malpresentations—including face or brow presentation, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique presentation—are organized as subtypes of malposition/malpresentation and are clinically relevant in the context of pregnancy, labor, and delivery management, especially for patients with a history of cesarean delivery and related uterine scarring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. Both the prolapsed arm and the transverse/oblique presentations are also classified as malposition; malpresentation, indicating that they are particular manifestations of fetal malposition. The Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين” (prolapsed fetal arm) is identified as the parent label for the prolapsed arm presentation, while the phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين” (instability of fetal position) is associated with the “has parent” relationship for malposition; malpresentation, reinforcing the concept that malposition reflects an unstable fetal orientation. In addition, the broader concept “Fetal presentation finding” is linked to malposition; malpresentation through a “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category
